flashcards.front: What are the six fundamental operations performed on data structures?
flashcards.back: The six fundamental operations are: (1) Traversal - visiting all elements exactly once, (2) Searching - finding the location of a target element, (3) Insertion - adding a new element, (4) Deletion - removing an existing element, (5) Sorting - arranging elements in a specific order, and (6) Merging - combining two or more ordered structures into one ordered structure.

flashcards.front: Derive the time complexity of Linear Search using recurrence relation.
flashcards.back: For Linear Search on n elements: T(n) = T(n-1) + 1. Unrolling: T(n) = T(n-k) + k. When k = n, T(n) = T(0) + n = n. Therefore, Time Complexity: O(n) — linear in the number of elements.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Binary Search and its derivation?
flashcards.back: Binary Search divides the search space by 2 each iteration. Recurrence: T(n) = T(n/2) + 1. Using Master's Theorem: a=1, b=2, f(n)=1 = Θ(n^0). Since log_b(a) = log_2(1) = 0, case 2 applies: T(n) = Θ(n^0 log n) = Θ(log n). Time Complexity: O(log n).

flashcards.front: Why is Binary Search faster than Linear Search mathematically?
flashcards.back: Linear Search: O(n) - compares up to n elements. Binary Search: O(log₂n) - halves search space each step. For n=1024: Linear worst-case = 1024 comparisons; Binary worst-case = log₂1024 = 10 comparisons. Binary search is exponentially faster for large datasets.

flashcards.front: Define the operation of Insertion in data structures.
flashcards.back: Insertion is the process of adding a new element to a data structure at a specified position. In arrays: requires shifting elements to maintain contiguity — O(n) worst case. In linked lists: O(1) if position known. In sorted arrays: must find correct position O(log n) then shift O(n).

flashcards.front: Define the operation of Deletion in data structures.
flashcards.back: Deletion is removing an existing element from a data structure. In arrays: requires shifting elements to fill the gap — O(n) worst case. In linked lists: O(1) if node pointer known, but requires traversal to find position. In hash tables: O(1) average case.

flashcards.front: Define the operation of Sorting and its classification.
flashcards.back: Sorting arranges elements in a specified order (ascending/descending). Classification: (1) Internal vs External - based on where data resides, (2) Comparison-based vs Distribution-based, (3) Stable vs Unstable - maintains relative order of equal elements, (4) Recursive vs Iterative algorithms.

flashcards.front: Define the operation of Merging in data structures.
flashcards.back: Merging combines two or more sorted sequences into a single sorted sequence. The merge operation assumes both input sequences are already sorted. Time complexity: O(n+m) where n and m are sizes of input sequences. Essential operation in merge sort.

flashcards.front: What is Traversal operation and its significance?
flashcards.back: Traversal accesses each element exactly once in a systematic manner. It is fundamental because many operations (searching, sorting, printing) require traversing all elements. Array traversal: O(n). Linked list traversal: O(n). Tree traversal: O(n) using DFS/BFS.

flashcards.front: Compare time complexities of operations on Array vs Linked List.
flashcards.back: Array: Search O(n), Insert O(n), Delete O(n), Random Access O(1). Linked List: Search O(n), Insert O(1) at head (O(n) to find position), Delete O(1) at head (O(n) to find position). Arrays have better cache locality; linked lists have dynamic size.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity analysis for recursive Binary Search?
flashcards.back: Binary Search has two implementations: (1) Iterative: Space O(1), (2) Recursive: Space O(log n) due to recursive call stack (each level stores return address and parameters). The recurrence stack depth equals the number of halvings.

flashcards.front: Explain best, average, and worst case complexities for Linear Search.
flashcards.back: Best Case: Element at first position → T(n) = 1 = O(1). Average Case: Element equally likely at any position → T(n) = (1+2+...+n)/n = (n+1)/2 = O(n). Worst Case: Element at last position or absent → T(n) = n = O(n).
